one_line_takeaway: Turning a fake-news detector's classification signal into structured natural-language critique (suspicious tokens + reasons + suggestions) rather than a scalar reward lets an LLM generator and a lightweight retrieval-augmented DeBERTa detector co-evolve stably without RL, yielding a detector that both beats static/non-adaptive training baselines on ROC-AUC and transfers better to an unseen external LLM attacker.
key_finding: RADAR beats both ablated training regimes (Standard Supervised Training: one-shot training on a static fake set of equal size; Static Adversarial Training: round-wise detector updates against a fixed, non-adapting generator) in every one of the 3 generator backbones x 2 retrieval settings tested. Main-table ROC-AUC (D-/D+): vs Qwen3-4B-Instruct 61.65/74.18 (RADAR) vs 57.41/67.64 (Standard) vs 55.44/71.36 (Static Adversarial); vs Gemma3-4B 59.67/75.13 (RADAR) vs 58.21/71.29 vs 51.13/68.30; vs Phi-3.5-mini 68.43/75.58 (RADAR) vs 58.59/64.10 vs 59.94/69.90. Zero-shot LLM judges (same three backbones used purely as prompted detectors, no training) are markedly weaker: 42.01-55.73 AUC without retrieval, best zero-shot result 68.39 (Phi-3.5-mini) with retrieval. Component ablation (Table 3) shows detector-side retrieval is the dominant single lever (e.g., 59.41->71.94 AUC for Qwen3 generator when only detector retrieval is added) versus a smaller standalone effect from generator-side retrieval alone (59.41->61.65); combining both is best (74.18). Feedback ablation (Table 5, dual-retrieval setting) shows removing VAF costs 1.58-2.97 AUC points, removing few-shot exemplars costs 2.26-4.02 points, and removing both costs up to 7.67 points, indicating the two feedback mechanisms are complementary rather than redundant. Cross-attacker transfer to the unseen GPT-5.3 Instant attacker (Table 6) shows RADAR still leads Standard/Static baselines under every generator backbone (e.g., 60.56/69.20 for RADAR vs Qwen3-trained detector, vs 58.52/58.76 Standard and 52.37/63.88 Static), evidencing that the co-evolution gain is not purely an artifact of overfitting to the specific training-time attacker distribution. Two supporting analyses in the appendix: (1) retrieval-overlap stratification (Table 7) shows retrieval gives ~0 AUC benefit when the top retrieved passage has ROUGE-L overlap <0.25 with the input (60.92 vs 60.88) but +9.4 to +10.8 AUC in higher-overlap bins, arguing gains are not solely a trivial near-duplicate lookup; (2) retrieval strategy/depth sweep (Table 8) shows DPR beats BM25 and peaks at k=3 (74.18 AUC) with a non-monotonic drop at k=5 (68.30 for DPR, 69.85 for BM25).
core_contribution: RADAR is an adversarial co-evolution framework for fake-news detection that couples (1) an LLM generator (attacker) that rewrites real news into factually-perturbed fakes via one of five randomly sampled manipulation strategies, and (2) a lightweight trainable DeBERTa-v3-base detector (defender). Retrieval (DPR + FAISS over an 811K-article news corpus) can be switched on independently for either side: generator-side retrieval supplies stylistic/realism priors for harder-to-detect fakes, while detector-side retrieval supplies external evidence for claim verification. The paper's central novel mechanism is Verbal Adversarial Feedback (VAF): instead of a scalar reward, the detector's judgment is converted into a structured natural-language critique -- suspicious tokens (from Integrated Gradients attribution), detection reasons, and improvement suggestions -- that is injected into the generator's prompt for the next round, alongside a cached few-shot bank of successful adversarial rewrites and periodic LoRA fine-tuning of the generator. This avoids policy-gradient/RL text-GAN optimization (e.g., SeqGAN-style) entirely.
threat_model: Attacker: an adaptive LLM-based rewriter that iteratively probes a deployed fake-news detector and refines its evasion strategy round over round, using the detector's own explanatory feedback (VAF) to target specific lexical/phrasing weaknesses -- i.e., a white/gray-box, feedback-driven adversarial text-generation attacker against a content-moderation classifier. A separate, disjoint external attacker (GPT-5.3 Instant, prompted independently, never seen in training) is used at evaluation time to test transfer to an attacker style outside the co-evolution loop. Defender: a lightweight, continually retrainable encoder classifier (not a frozen prompted LLM judge) intended to keep pace with an evolving attacker at low inference cost (single forward pass plus top-k retrieval, k=3).
limitations: Paper's own stated limitation: evaluation is confined to a single benchmark, AdvFake-News-Please, deliberately chosen because it is post-2021 (to reduce train/test knowledge overlap with the DeBERTa-v3-base pretraining cutoff, ~early 2021) and pairs real/fake news with a compatible, similarly-dated retrieval corpus -- a combination the authors say very few public benchmarks satisfy, so cross-dataset generalization (e.g., to FakeNewsNet, BuzzFeed, LIAR) is left to future work. Additional limitations not flagged as such but evident from the text: absolute ROC-AUC values remain modest even for the best RADAR configuration (peak 75.58, i.e., far from a solved detection problem); the cross-attacker transfer test uses only one external attacker model (GPT-5.3 Instant) prompted with a fairly permissive fact-altering instruction, not a red-team-optimized adversary; retrieval depth is non-monotonic and was tuned on this single dataset/corpus, so the reported k=3 optimum may not transfer; the detector-training batch size is very small (2), and no statistical significance testing or variance across seeds is reported for the ROC-AUC numbers in Tables 2-8. The paper explicitly discusses dual-use risk in its Ethical Considerations section: the same generator/feedback machinery used to strengthen the detector could be repurposed to craft more deceptive misinformation, and the authors state they position RADAR strictly as a defensive pipeline and do not advocate deploying the generator for content creation.

datasets_used: AdvFake-News-Please (Chen et al., 2025) -- real articles from News-Please plus paired adversarial fakes, used as the primary train/eval benchmark
~811K-article public news corpus (same date range as AdvFake-News-Please) used as the DPR/FAISS retrieval index, with exact seed articles removed and near-duplicates filtered
Held-out cross-attacker evaluation set generated by prompting GPT-5.3 Instant (OpenAI, 2026) as an unseen external attacker, used only at evaluation time
quantitative_results: Main results (ROC-AUC %, D-/D+): RADAR vs Qwen3-4B-Instruct 61.65/74.18; vs Gemma3-4B 59.67/75.13; vs Phi-3.5-mini(3.8B) 68.43/75.58
Zero-shot LLM detector baselines (no retrieval): Qwen3-4B-Instruct 55.73, Gemma3-4B 49.40, Phi-3.5-mini(3.8B) 42.01; with retrieval: 59.43, 55.94, 68.39 respectively
Retrieval-component ablation (D detector fixed, Qwen3 generator): No-retrieval 59.41 -> Generator-only retrieval 61.65 -> Detector-only retrieval 71.94 -> Both 74.18
Feedback ablation under dual-retrieval (RADAR full/w-o VAF/w-o Few-shot/w-o both), ROC-AUC: Qwen3 74.18/71.22/70.80/66.51; Gemma3 75.13/73.55/72.87/72.49; Phi-3.5 75.58/72.61/71.56/71.19

Cross-attacker transfer (RADAR, D-/D+): vs Qwen3-trained detector 60.56/69.20; vs Gemma3 60.28/69.35; vs Phi-3.5 61.21/69.59, each beating Standard Supervised and Static Adversarial baselines under the same generator backbone
Retrieval strategy/depth sweep (ROC-AUC): BM25 k=1/3/5 = 59.84/71.15/69.85; DPR k=1/3/5 = 66.54/74.18/68.30
baselines_compared: Zero-shot prompted LLM judges (Qwen3-4B-Instruct, Gemma3-4B, Phi-3.5-mini 3.8B) used directly as detectors without fine-tuning
Standard Supervised Training -- DeBERTa-v3-base detector trained once on a static fake set produced by a fixed (non-adapting) generator, matched in total size to RADAR's training data

Static Adversarial Training -- detector updated round-by-round against a fixed generator (no generator-side adaptation), isolating the contribution of detector-only continual updates versus RADAR's full co-evolution
